title: Cost-Share Accountability Act of 2025
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.
plain_summary: This law is about making sure the government watches how it spends money on energy projects. Usually, private companies have to pay part of the cost when they work with the government on energy research and projects. Sometimes the government decides companies don't have to pay their share. This new law says the Department of Energy must write reports every three months about when they let companies off the hook for paying. These reports have to be sent to Congress and shared with the public so everyone knows what's happening with taxpayer money.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Cost-Share Accountability Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Reporting Requirements
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Energy Policy Act of two thousand five. It adds new rules about reporting to Section nine hundred eighty-eight of that law. The new rule says the Secretary of Energy must create reports about how the Department of Energy uses its power to reduce or eliminate cost-sharing requirements. Cost-sharing requirements mean rules that say private companies or other groups must pay part of the costs for energy projects that the government also helps fund. The Secretary must send the first report within one hundred twenty days after this law becomes official. After that, the Secretary must send a new report at least every three months. These reports must go to four committees in Congress: the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology and the Committee on Appropriations in the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources and the Committee on Appropriations in the Senate. The Secretary must also make these reports available to the public. Each report must explain how the Department used its authority to reduce or eliminate cost-sharing requirements during the time period covered by that report.
